coredata.dc:title: A multilevel fluorometric biosensor based on boric acid embedded in carbon dots to detect intracellular and serum glucose
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Qinghua
Jiang, Junhao
Chen, Zhiqiong
Han, Chuyi
Zhang, Xianming
Yang, Shiyu
Zhou, Ping
Deng, Tao
Yu, Chao
coredata.dc:description: Diabetes mellitus is one of the most common chronic diseases, and blood glucose monitoring benefits for diagnosis and management of diabetic mellitus. In this paper, a multimodal photoluminescence (PL) bio-platform, R-CDs/B2O3, with solid-state fluorescence, was developed by dispersing Rhodamine B carbon dots (R-CDs) in the matrix of boric acid (BA) as a simple, cheap agent for the visual assay of intracellular and serum glucose concentration, which was based on BA can specially bound with 1, 2-diol of glucose. The good linear relationship between the PL spectra of the R-CDs/B2O3 and glucose concentration indicated that R-CDs/B2O3 could dynamically monitor glucose concentration in vitro. Furthermore, the fluorescent color variations of R-CDs/B2O3 gel were monitored by the UV lamp and the green channel intensities of the colored images were processed by using the RGB option of a smartphone, manifesting that R-CDs/B2O3 could be used as a portable test gel for serum glucose. Significantly, it is convenient for self-monitoring of blood glucose for diabetic patients. The method was also successfully used for the assay of glucose in blood serum. Hence, the proposed fluorescence sensor opens the door for further research and development of more reliable non-enzymatic assays for continuous glucose monitoring systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Boric acid
Fluorescence sensor
Test gel
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The binding of the R-CDs/B2O3 composite to the glucose
Detecting intracellular glucose levels using R-CDs/B2O3 in B16F10 Cancer Cells
Preparation of portable test kits and visual detection of glucose concentration
Fluorescence probe R-CDs/B2O3 detection glucose in serum
Synthesis of R-CDs and R-CDs/B2O3 composite
The specific binding of R-CDs/B2O3 to the glucose
Detecting intracellular glucose levels using R-CDs/B2O3 in B16F10 cancer cells
Preparation of portable gel and visual detection of glucose concentration
Fluorescence probe R-CDs/B2O3 detection glucose in serum

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Introduction Teachers Foundation of Chongqing Medical University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2019M653356), Chongqing Postdoctoral Science Foundation (cstc2019jcyjbshX0060), the Introduction Teachers Foundation of Chongqing Medical University (YXY2018BSH1), and the Innovation research group in Colleges and Universities Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (No. CXQT20012).

This work was financially supported by the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2 019M65335 6 ), Chongqing Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( c stc2019jcyjbshX006 0 ), the Introduction Teachers Foundation of Chongqing Medical University ( Y XY2018BSH 1 ), and the Innovation research group in Colleges and Universities Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (No. C XQT2001 2 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption of R-CDs, B2O3 and R-CDs/B2O3. (b) The excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs/B2O3 in an aqueous solution. Insert: The images of solid R-CDs/B2O3 under white light, and different phases (solid and aqueous solution) of R-CDs/B2O3 under UV lamp with excitation wavelength at 365 nm, respectively. (c) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs/B2O3 aqueous solution under excitation at different wavelengths. (d) Fluorescence decay curve of R-CDs/B2O3 in water.

(a) TEM image of R-CDs/B2O3. Scale bars= 20 nm. Insert image: the size distribution of R-CDs/B2O3 characterized by TEM results. (b) The zeta potential of R-CDs and R-CDs/B2O3. (c) FT-IR spectra of R-CDs, R-CDs/B2O3. (d) XPS spectrum of R-CDs/B2O3. (e) and (f) XPS high-resolution C 1s and B 1s spectra of R-CDs/B2O3, respectively.

(a) Selectivity for glucose detection by PL spectra with excitation wavelength at 450 nm. The glucose concentration is 10 mM and the other interference species are 5 mM. (b) The PL spectra of R-CDs/B2O3 binding with different concentrations of glucose with excitation wavelength at 450 nm. (c) The linear calibration plot of green color intensity versus the concentration of glucose.
Detecting intracellular glucose levels using R-CDs/B2O3 in B16F10 Cancer Cells. Scale bars = 100 µm.

(a) The scheme of gel of R-CDs/B2O3. (b) The images of gel reaction with glucose under UV lamp with excitation wavelength at 365 nm and the green channel intensities of the colored images were processed by using the RGB option of a smartphone. (c) The image of gel reaction with serum diluted in different multiples.

(a) The schedule of R-CDs/B2O3 detecting blood glucose. (b) The PL spectra of R-CDs/B2O3 detection glucose in blood serum samples. (c) The actual value of glucose in different blood serum samples with glucose meter measured.

The preparation of boric acid functionalized carbon dots, R-CDs/B2O3, and the schedule of R-CDs/B2O3 binding with glucose and preparation of portable test kits and visual detection of glucose concentration.
Detection of glucose in mice serum samples.

head.ce:title: A multilevel fluorometric biosensor based on boric acid embedded in carbon dots to detect intracellular and serum glucose

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Diabetes mellitus is one of the most common chronic diseases, and blood glucose monitoring benefits for diagnosis and management of diabetic mellitus. In this paper, a multimodal photoluminescence (PL) bio-platform, R-CDs/B2O3, with solid-state fluorescence, was developed by dispersing Rhodamine B carbon dots (R-CDs) in the matrix of boric acid (BA) as a simple, cheap agent for the visual assay of intracellular and serum glucose concentration, which was based on BA can specially bound with 1, 2-diol of glucose. The good linear relationship between the PL spectra of the R-CDs/B2O3 and glucose concentration indicated that R-CDs/B2O3 could dynamically monitor glucose concentration in vitro. Furthermore, the fluorescent color variations of R-CDs/B2O3 gel were monitored by the UV lamp and the green channel intensities of the colored images were processed by using the RGB option of a smartphone, manifesting that R-CDs/B2O3 could be used as a portable test gel for serum glucose. Significantly, it is convenient for self-monitoring of blood glucose for diabetic patients. The method was also successfully used for the assay of glucose in blood serum. Hence, the proposed fluorescence sensor opens the door for further research and development of more reliable non-enzymatic assays for continuous glucose monitoring systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes mellitus is one of the most common chronic diseases and it is estimated that the population of diabetes will increase to 693 million adults by 2045 [1–3]. Diagnosis of diabetes can be reflected by blood glucose concentrations higher or lower than the normal range of 80–120 mg/dL (4.4–6.6 mM) [4,5]. And abnormal glucose levels in human blood can result in several disorders such as blindness, nerve degeneration and kidney failure [6]. Hence, the diagnosis and management of diabetic patients and other diseases related to glucose metabolism need precise monitoring and control of the glucose level in the body. Given the significance of sensing glucose, a large number of studies have emphasized glucose sensors [7,8]. Now, glucose selective agents can be classified into two systems: one is based on the strategy of using glucose oxidase (GOx) hydrolysate to monitor Glucose. For instance, the sensing strategy through detecting the hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with GOx has been developed for the detection of glucose since H2O2 is a product of glucose/GOx reaction [9–11]. The other method for the determination of glucose is a system without the enzyme by designing and synthesizing agents for glucose [12–14]. Nevertheless, the application of enzyme-based glucose sensors was limited owing to the instability of the enzyme, the high cost and degradation of GOx [15]. Hence, non-enzymatic systems, especially fluorescent probes, have been extensively received attention because of their higher stability, lower cost and easier preparation [16,17].

Carbon dots (CDs), as a group of newly emerged fluorescent nanomaterials, have a wide range of applications, including drug delivery, photodynamic therapy, photocatalysis and electrocatalysis [18,19]. In addition, CDs are attracting considerable attention and becoming both an important versatile platform for biosensors because of the excellent fluorescence stability and the tunable fluorescence emission [20,21]. Shan et al. have developed two fluorescence CDs used for the detection of ROS and H2O2, respectively [22,23]. Undoubtedly, it is important for the CDs-based sensor that specific binding of CDs to the substance. Chemical modification of CDs is generally necessary for more effectively sensing. Based on the surface chemical properties of CDs, the compounds could be attached to the surface of the CDs by physical adsorption or functional group reaction [24]. However, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased during the modification, or even the fluorescence would be quenched when the CDs are in solid-state owing to the aggregation induced quenching (AIQ) effect, which limited the application of CDs in sensing and test kit. Interestingly, the AIQ effect of CDs was solved by introducing an inorganic matrix, which can greatly reduce the energy gap between the singlet and triplet state and the formed glassy state can effectively protect the excited triplet states of CDs from nonradiative deactivation [25]. Hence, to endow CDs with more functions, it is necessary to find a suitable matrix to modify CDs.

Boron is like carbon in the aspect of its capability to form stable covalently bonded molecular networks. In parallel with the development of carbon nanomaterials, boron nanomaterials have been researched extensively [26]. Low molecular weight BA compounds have found important applications because of their unique electronic and physicochemical properties [27,28]. The application of boron-containing compounds, such as boron neutron capture therapy (BNCT) agents, drug carriers, and saccharide sensing agents have been reviewed previously [29,30]. Especially, the fluorescence of CDs was retained in solid form by BA modification which was convenient for the preparation of portable gel to sense. Moreover, based on BA can specially bound with 1, 2- and 1, 3-diol, BA functionalized CDs were investigated for carbohydrate sensing [31,32]. Compared with conventional glucose-responsive materials–enzymes, BA polymers benefit from their stability, durability, low cost, and high glucose sensitivity and are widely used in glucose identification materials [33].

Here in, we successfully synthesized a kind of BA functionalized nanocomposites with yellow-green fluorescence, called R-CDs/B2O3, which was achieved by dispersing R-CDs in the matrix of BA. Moreover, R-CDs/B2O3 was allowed as a multimodal PL bio-platform to detect intracellular and serum glucose concentration ( Scheme 1). The intensity of fluorescence would get weaker regularly with the increase of glucose concentration, demonstrated that R-CDs/B2O3 had an excellent ability of dynamic monitoring glucose in vitro. Furthermore, the fluorescence of R-CDs/B2O3 gel remains remarkably responsive to glucose concentration by using the RGB option of a smartphone. In addition, the ability to monitor glucose was further demonstrated by detecting serum glucose concentration.
All chemical lists including other materials are given in the Supplementary Information.

The R-CDs with yellow-green fluorescence were synthesized by hydrothermal pyrolysis of RhB at 180 °C for 8 h in NaOH aqueous solution. In brief, 96 mg RhB was dissolved in 15 mL NaOH aqueous solution (0.67 M), and then the solution was transferred into poly (tetrafluoroethylene) lined autoclaves and heated at 180 °C in an oven for 8 h. After cooling down to 25 °C naturally, the resultant orange mixture solution was precipitated by HCl. Then sediment was obtained under centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 3 min and the brown solid R-CDs were obtained by drying 3 h at 60 °C. The fluorescence QY of R-CDs was measured at the excitation wavelength of 495 nm by using the following equation:
Qb = Qa ×
Fb
Fa
×
Aa
Ab

Here, Q is the quantum yield, and F refers to the integrated fluorescence intensity. A refers to the absorbance at the same wavelength. The sample is presented with b and a represents the standard reference. RhB aqueous solution is used as a standard reference (QY: 0.89).

Firstly, 2 mg R-CDs and 3 g BA were dissolved in the flask with 40 mL ultrapure water by ultrasonic for 30 min. Then solvent of the mixture solution was removed by rotary evaporation under 60 °C for 30 min and the flaxen solid was obtained in the round-bottom flask. Next, the flash was heated in an oven at 180 °C for 5 h, after cooling down to 25 °C naturally. The dialysis method was adopted to remove the unbound BA before the final product was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The binding of the R-CDs/B2O3 composite to the glucose
ce:section.ce:para: Lactose, fructose, galactose, sucrose, dopamine, glycine, uric acid and ascorbic acid were selected to evaluate the selectivity of R-CDs/B2O3 toward glucose sensing. Each of the 2 mL tubes containing 900 μL R-CDs/B2O3 solutions and 100 μL various interference were ultrasonic for 5 min at 25 °C. In addition, the mixtures of R-CDs/B2O3, glucose and different interferences were obtained with a ratio of 9: 0.5: 0.5. Their fluorescence responses were measured by an RF-5301 spectrofluorophotometer. The responses to glucose and the interference on R-CDs/B2O3 were compared.

Different concentrations of glucose were prepared with 0 mM, 1 mM, 2 mM, 4 mM, 8 mM, 10 mM, 12 mM, 14 mM, 16 mM, 18 mM and 20 mM. 100 μL different concentrations of glucose were mixed with 900 μL R-CDs/B2O3 composite solution (5 mg/mL), respectively. Then the mixture was formed by ultrasonic for 5 min at 25 °C. The fluorescence was measured by an RF-5301 spectrofluorophotometer.

The cytotoxicity of R-CDs/B2O3 was evaluated by MTT assay using mouse melanoma cell B16F10. Mainly, B16F10 cells were seeded on 96-well plates and cultured in 1640 medium with 10% (v/v) calf serum at 37 °C and in 5% CO2 for 24 h. Then the cells were incubated for 24 h using fresh 1640 medium with different concentrations of R-CDs/B2O3 (0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 125, 250, 500 and 1000 μg/mL), respectively. Then 100 μL fresh medium containing 10 μL (5 mg/mL in sterile PBS) MTT solution was added to each well and the cells were incubated for 4 h. Afterward, the culture medium containing MTT was discarded and cells were lysed with 150 μL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) at 37 °C for 30 min in the drying oven. The absorbance of the solution was recorded at a wavelength of 490 nm with a microplate reader. The relative cell viability (%) related to the blank control were calculated by using the following equation:
Cell
viability ( % ) =
test
sample
blank
control
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting intracellular glucose levels using R-CDs/B2O3 in B16F10 Cancer Cells
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence images of mouse melanoma cells B16F10 incubated with R-CDs/B2O3 without glucose in the growth media were examined. Briefly, melanoma cells B16F10 were first seeded on 24-well plates and cultured in 1640 medium with 10% (v/v) calf serum at 37 °C and in 5% CO2 for 24 h. After incubation, the media were replaced with a fresh 1640 medium containing 200 μL of R-CDs/B2O3 (50 µg/mL). After 4 h, the cells were washed with fresh PBS three times and further cultured with sugar-free 1640 medium containing different concentrations of glucose solution (5 mM, 10 mM and 20 mM), respectively. Moreover, both without adding R-CDs/B2O3 group and without adding the glucose group as two control groups. At last, the cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 20 min and washed by PBS (pH 7.4) three times. Before imaging by an inverted fluorescence microscope, the cells were mixed further with DAPI for 10 min at 4 °C for twice to stain the nuclei and the cells were washed by fresh PBS (pH 7.4) four times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of portable test kits and visual detection of glucose concentration
ce:section.ce:para: Polypropylene centrifuge tubes (1.5 mL) were used to prepare the portable test gel for glucose concentration. 60 mg agarose was completely dissolved in 6 mL boiling water, stirring for 15 min. When the solution cooled to 40 °C, the 1.8 mL R-CDs/B2O3 composite solution was added. After uniform blending, the mixture (100 μL) was transferred into the cap of the centrifuge tube. The hydrogels were shaped after 5 min under 25 °C. Then 500 μL of glucose with different concentrations were added into the test gel, respectively. After closing the cap, the test gel was turned upside down to allow the infiltration of the glucose solution into the hydrogels and incubated at 25 °C for 30 min. The color of the hydrogel was recorded by an ultraviolet lamp at 356 nm. Then the green channel intensities of the colored images were processed by using the RGB option of a smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence probe R-CDs/B2O3 detection glucose in serum
ce:section.ce:para: The ability to detect glucose concentration for R-CDs/B2O3 was evaluated using mouse serum. Briefly, fresh whole blood obtained from three KM mice was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min, and then serum concentrates from the supernatant were collected in different centrifuge tubes as a detected sample. Every group containing 100 μL of serum and 900 μL of R-CDs/B2O3 reacted for 5 min under ultrasound. In addition, it only contains R-CDs/B2O3 or serum group as a control group. Finally, the PL spectra of three groups were obtained with an excitation wavelength located at 450 nm. Furthermore, the actual value of three groups of serum was measured with a commercial glucometer (YASEE, type: GLM-73).

The fresh whole blood obtained from the same KM mouse was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min, and then serum concentrate was collected in the supernatant. The serum was diluted at dilution ratios of 0, 1, 1.5, 2 and 2.5. Then 500 μL of different concentration serum samples were respectively added to the test gel. PBS group and only includes R-CDs/B2O3 group as a control. The other operations were the same as before. Meanwhile, time-resolved PL of R-CDs/B2O3 with different concentrations of serum were obtained by monitoring the fluorescence lifetime.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of R-CDs and R-CDs/B2O3 composite
ce:section.ce:para: A yellow-green R-CDs/B2O3 was successfully prepared to be used as a fluorescent probe to detect glucose. Firstly, R-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal pyrolysis of RhB at 180 °C for 8 h in the aqueous solution of NaOH (0.67 M), which refers to the previous literature [34]. As the TEM images depicted in Fig. S1, R-CDs show the spherical shape. The images of solid R-CDs under white light, solid R-CDs and R-CDs aqueous solution under UV lamp with excitation wavelength at 365 nm were recorded, respectively (Fig. S2). Distinctly, the color of solid R-CDs under white light is brown, and the color of solid R-CDs is also brown and have rarely fluorescence under UV lamp, however, R-CDs aqueous solution shows consumingly yellow-green fluorescence, indicating that the AIQ effect occurs when the CDs is in solid-state. Moreover, to measure fluorescence quantum yields (QYs), RhB aqueous solution has been used as a standard agent. According to the PL spectra of RhB and R-CDs in Fig. S3, calculations results showed that compared with the QY of RhB (0.86), the R-CDs sample has increased to 6.70, demonstrating that R-CDs were found to display high QYs.

Further, the matrix, BA, was introduced to solve the AIQ effect R-CDs. Firstly, the fluorescence property was characterized by UV–vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectrophotometer in Fig. 1. In the absorption spectrum (Fig. 1a), two strong peaks of at around 440 nm and 470 nm belong to characteristic absorption peaks of RhB, as well as two weak bands at around 312 nm and 380 nm are exhibited, corresponding to the π-π * transition of CC [35], large amounts of sp2-bonded carbon and n-π * transition of CO [36], respectively. Compared with R-CDs, R-CDs/B2O3 also maintains characteristic absorption peaks of RhB, whereas the peak at around 388 nm disappears, ascribing to the successful introduction of BA. Interestingly, there is no obvious absorption peak of B2O3 alone. Meanwhile, the excitation and emission wavelength of R-CDs/B2O3 ascertained by PL spectra is located at 450 nm and 513 nm in Fig. 1b. As can be seen from insert image in Fig. 1b, the R-CDs/B2O3 powder is white under light, and the powder under UV lamp display significantly yellow fluorescence, which is different from R-CDs, indicating that BA modification could solve the AIQ effect of the R-CDs. As a common inorganic matrix, BA, which could restrain solid-state intermolecular interactions of R-CDs and the formed glassy state could effectively protect the excited triplet states of CDs from nonradiative deactivation [25,37]. However, there is no significant difference of fluorescence images in UV lamp for R-CDs/B2O3 aqueous solution compared with R-CDs aqueous solution. In addition, as shown in Fig. 1c, the maximum fluorescence emission is still at 513 nm with the excitation wavelengths changing from 360 nm to 480 nm before (Fig. S4) and after (Fig. 1c) R-CDs are modified with BA, which further illustrates that BA makes no difference to the fluorescence of R-CDs and displays excitation wavelength-independent emission prosperity. Interestingly, R-CDs/B2O3 appears a new emission peak located at around 541 nm compared with R-CDs (Fig. 1c). Subsequently, to further confirm the origin of this peak, we investigate the PL spectra of B2O3 alone. As shown in Fig. S5, a different level redshift is observed for the PL spectra of B2O3 under different excitation wavelengths. Importantly, the emission peak of B2O3 (λex = 450 nm) is located at 520 nm. According to the reference reported, the formation of chemical bonds would dramatically improve their photophysical properties by red-shifting spectra and increasing the Stokes shift [38]. Fig. S6 intuitively manifests that the strong peak of R-CDs/B2O3 at 513 nm belongs to R-CDs, which is not affected by the introduction of B2O3. The weak peak of R-CDs/B2O3 at 541 nm is attributed to the redshift of the B2O3 characterization peak. The above results manifested that the PL spectra of R-CDs/B2O3 belongs to the overlap of B2O3 peak and R-CDs peak. And Fig. S7 displays that the fluorescence intensity and emission peak of R-CDs/B2O3 with different batches have no distinct change and the corresponding images of R-CDs/B2O3 display yellow solid fluorescence, demonstrating that R-CDs/B2O3 has an excellent repeatability. Interestingly, the intensity of solid fluorescence has a visible difference due to different synthesis and storage periods. The PL lifetime values of the R-CDs/B2O3 were then determined in Fig. 1d. R-CDs/B2O3 exhibits a longer PL lifetime and is up to 30 ns.

The structure and chemical composition of the R-CDs/B2O3 was confirmed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) in Fig. 2. TEM image (Fig. 2a) suggested that the morphology of R-CDs is uniform, and the particles are well separated from one another in a spherical shape, and the size distribution characterized by TEM results is 1.75 nm ± 0.75 nm. The zeta potential of R-CDs is −0.5 ± 0.06 mV, whereas R-CDs/B2O3 is −10.4 ± 1.1 mV due to the overlap of negative charges on R-CDs and B2O3 with oxygen anion, which was associated with the success of R-CDs/B2O3 construction (Fig. 2b). Furthermore, FT-IR further revealed the base structure of the R-CDs and R-CDs/B2O3. As shown in Fig. 2c, the characteristic peak located at about 3226 cm−1 and 1490 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H and B-O, respectively [39]. The band peaked at 1189 cm−1 is originated from an asymmetrically stretched oxygen atom connecting the trigonal boron atoms. The new band at 791 cm−1 is attributed to the characteristic boron-carbon bond [40]. These results agree with the XPS analysis. The elements of R-CDs/B2O3 were surveyed by the XPS spectrum in Fig. 2d. It is clearly shown the presence of C，N, O and B. The HR C 1s spectrum of R-CDs/B2O3 in Fig. 1e exhibit three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV and 288.9 eV, which are attributed to sp2 C, C-O and O-CO, respectively. The HR B 1s spectra of R-CDs/B2O3 in Fig. 1f can be fitted to two bands centered at 192.4 eV, 194.1 eV, which belong to BO, B-O, respectively [41]. The above results indicate that electron-withdrawing boron atoms and the formation of boron-carbon bonds were introduced successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The specific binding of R-CDs/B2O3 to the glucose
ce:section.ce:para: The ability to specific binding with the glucose was investigated through the fluorescence color of the as-prepared R-CDs/B2O3. To determine the selectivity of a fluorometric assay based on R-CDs/B2O3 to glucose, the PL spectra of R-CDs/B2O3 before and after adding glucose or other interfering species were tested. As shown in Fig. 3a, the fluorescence intensity is apparent lower when R-CDs/B2O3 bind to glucose specifically compared with the presence of lactose, fructose, galactose, sucrose, dopamine, glycine, uric acid and ascorbic acid, indicating that no significant interference was observed when interfering species existed, and reveals that the R-CDs/B2O3 could be used as a highly selective fluorescence probe. In addition, Fig. S8 showed that the PL intensity of the mixture of glucose and different interference species is equivalent to that of glucose alone, further demonstrating the ability of R-CDs/B2O3 to detect glucose. R-CDs/B2O3 could specially bind with glucose, ascribing to the formation of boron ester between oxygen anion on the B2O3 and the 1, 2-cis-diol of glucose. It was reported that the spatial separation of two boronic acid moieties in derivatives had a significant impact on which saccharide is bound [42], resulting in different binding strengths between R-CDs/B2O3 and those substances. Except for steric configuration, the interaction between R-CDs/B2O3 and samples was also relative to the pH. The pH of as-prepared R-CDs/B2O3 is around 6.5 which is more beneficial for interaction with glucose compared with other interference species. Consequently, R-CDs/B2O3 had an excellent capability of specific binding to glucose.

Next, the detailed emission titration experiments of R-CDs/B2O3 with various concentrations of glucose were carried out. As shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence intensity regular decreases with the glucose concentration increasing from 1 mM to 20 mM. We further investigated the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs/B2O3 and the concentration of glucose at a suitable reaction time. and the result shown in Fig. 3c indicated that a good linear correlation between green color intensity and glucose concentration is obtained within the concentration ranged from 2 mM to 10 mM with a correlation coefficient (r) of 0.983. Therefore, we conjecture preliminarily that the R-CDs/B2O3 has the ability to dynamic monitoring glucose concentration in vitro. Further, this quenching phenomenon of fluorescence was studied. Quenching phenomenon of fluorescence attributed to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) effect [43]: BA matrix inhibited intramolecular electron transfer of R-CDs, as a result, the synthesized R-CD/B2O3 had excellent fluorescence. Nevertheless, the oxygen anion on the R-CDs/B2O3 specifically binds to the 1, 2- cis-diol group on glucose and further the complex with boron ester was formed, which destroyed the B-C bond, resulting in electron transfer within the R-CDs and fluorescence final decreased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting intracellular glucose levels using R-CDs/B2O3 in B16F10 cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the ability to detect glucose levels, B16F10 cancer cells were selected as a model. Topically, the MTT assay was carried out before detecting intracellular glucose levels to assess the biocompatibility safety of R-CDs/B2O3. As seen in Fig. S9, the R-CDs/B2O3 were nearly nontoxic to B16F10 cells when the concentrations of R-CDs/B2O3 varying between 5 μg/mL and 50 μg/mL. The concentration at 50 μg/mL was the optimal concentration to follow the study. The toxicity is still low, even though the concentrations of R-CDs/B2O3 surpass 50 μg/mL. In addition, the biocompatibility of R-CDs/B2O3 was also evaluated in HUVEC (Fig. S10), demonstrating that R-CDs/B2O3 has good biocompatibility in vitro.

As a fluorescence probe, the ability to detect intracellular glucose levels was studied. B16F10 cells labeled with the R-CDs/B2O3 could be simply optically differentiated by using a fluorescence microscope. As shown in Fig. 4, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs/B2O3 regular decreased when feeding on different concentrations of 0 mM, 5 mM, 10 mM and 20 mM glucose, respectively. Quantitative analysis of detecting intracellular glucose levels show in Fig. S11 and the fluorescence intensity highlighted feeding on 0 mM of glucose. B16F10 cells without R-CDs/B2O3 were treated as a control. Moreover, the merged images indicated that the R-CDs/B2O3 was in the cytoplasm. In conclusion, R-CDs/B2O3 was an alternative biosensor to detect intracellular glucose levels in vitro.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of portable gel and visual detection of glucose concentration
ce:section.ce:para: To realize rapid and visualized assay of the concentration of glucose, which is convenient for self-monitoring of blood glucose for diabetic patients, a portable gel was designed by utilizing agarose hydrogel as a visual detection platform. Following the advantage of negligible background color and fluorescence emission as well as large loading capacity and controllable shape, the hydrogel is considered as a particularly suitable material to design the visual detection platform [44]. The schema of preparation and detection procedure for portable gel showed in Fig. 5a. The test gel was applied to assay the concentration of glucose and the results were shown in Fig. 5b. Further, the color of hydrogel recorded by the smartphone gradually faded from the initial green under UV lamp with excitation wavelength at 365 nm when the concentration of glucose increased from 0 mM to 20 mM (hyperglycemia is defined as blood glucose above 10 mM). In addition, the green channel intensities of the colored images were processed by using the RGB option of mobile software called Color Picker. In addition, the serum samples diluted into different multiples were also used to verify the ability of visual detection glucose for the gel of R-CDs/B2O3. As shown in Fig. 5c, compared with the control group, there is no obvious change in color for the PBS group, however, the color of gel faded evidently after adding serum of which the amount serum is the same as PBS. Moreover, the fluorescence of gel decreases with serum concentration increased. Therefore, the portable gel could be readily used as a visual detection platform and applicable for detecting glucose concentration without complicated sample pretreatment and sophisticated instruments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence probe R-CDs/B2O3 detection glucose in serum
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the practical applicability of R-CDs/B2O3 prepared in this study, R-CDs/B2O3 was used for the assay of glucose in blood serum. The schedule of R-CDs/B2O3 detecting glucose in blood serum was shown in Fig. 6a. The detection of glucose in the blood serum samples obtained from three different KM mice was carried out. The result was shown in Fig. 6b, indicating that the PL spectra in the mixture of blood serum and R-CDs/B2O3 were far below that of R-CDs/B2O3 alone. Subsequently, to further investigate the detecting mechanism, the PL decay traces of R-CDs/B2O3 were also collected before and after the mixture with different concentrations of serum. The lifetime of CDs is usually one of the most useful methods to distinguish static quenching from dynamic quenching by measuring the fluorescence lifetime in the absence and presence of quencher molecules [45]. The PL lifetime affected by quencher belongs to dynamic quenching and the opposite is static quenching. Fig. S12 showed that the PL lifetimes of the R-CDs/B2O3 containing different concentration serum are equivalent to that of R-CDs/B2O3 alone, indicating that the quenching process is static quenching. Specifically, the excited fluorescence molecules (R-CDs/B2O3) formed an effective quenched spherical model or complex after mixing glucose, resulting in the fluorescence dramatic decrease. The above phenomenon was consistent with the PET effect and both demonstrated from two different aspects that the formation of the complex between R-CDs/B2O3 and glucose resulted in a final decrease in fluorescence.

In addition, we also compared the ability of R-CDs/B2O3 to detect glucose with a commercial glucometer. The actual values of glucose in different blood serum samples were recorded by a glucometer in Fig. 6c. Furthermore, measured values (6.12 mM, 8.41 mM and 6.22 mM) calculated according to standard curve results were close to actual value (6.4 mM, 8.8 mM and 6.1 mM) of serum calculated by commercial glucometer in three samples ( Table 1).

In summary, we have successfully fabricated a multimodal PL bio-platform, R-CDs/B2O3, with yellow-green fluorescence by dispersing R-CDs in the matrix of BA. Further, we found that R-CDs/B2O3 not only could dynamically monitor the intracellular and serum glucose concentration but also could as a portable test gel to detect the glucose levels and the color of gel further was recorded by using the RGB option of a smartphone. In addition, the ability to detect glucose concentration was confirmed by blood serum samples. Hence, the method we proposed could be convenient for self-monitoring of blood glucose for diabetic patients. Significantly, the multimodal PL bio-platform was designed as an analytical method of surveying the molecular basis of physiological and pathological events.

Qinghua Yu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Zhiqiong Chen: Investigation, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Junhao Jiang: Methodology, Validation, Supervision. Chuyi Han: Investigation, Methodology, Resources. Xianming Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Resources. Shiyu Yang: Investigation, Resources. Ping Zhou: Investigation, Resources. Tao Deng: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Chao Yu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2019M653356), Chongqing Postdoctoral Science Foundation (cstc2019jcyjbshX0060), the Introduction Teachers Foundation of Chongqing Medical University (YXY2018BSH1), and the Innovation research group in Colleges and Universities Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (No. CXQT20012).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.130898.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qinghua Yu, a graduate student, now is studying College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University.

Junhao Jiang received his master's degree in pharmacochemistry from Chongqing Medical University, and now is working at the college of pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University. His researches focus on designing and screening of lead compounds.

Zhiqiong Chen received his master's degree in Environmental Chemistry from Nankai University. She graduated from Chongqing Medical University, majoring in pharmacology, with a doctorate degree. Her researches focus on analysis of food, drugs, chiral drugs and drugs in vivo.
Chuyi Han, a graduate student, now is studying College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University, and her paper was published in Carbon.
Xianming Zhang, a graduate student, now is studying College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University.
Shiyu Yang, a graduate student, now is studying College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University.
Ping Zhou, a graduate student, now is studying College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University.

Tao Deng received his Pharm.D from China Pharmaceutical University, and work at college of pharmacy in Chongqing Medical University. His researches focus on designing and constructing the theranostic platform based on quantum dots.
Chao Yu is the professor of the college of pharmacy in Chongqing Medical University. His researches focus on mechanism of vascular injury and the detection of disease markers.
